level.name: Beginner — Dai-ikkyo (Throws 1-8)
level.description: Foundation: ukemi, kuzushi, tai-sabaki, and the first 8 Gokyo throws.

techniques.name: De-ashi-harai
techniques.description: Timing-based forward-movement foot sweep; Intercepting a step; Teaching distance, kuzushi, and rhythm
avoidWhen: Do not force it when uke's weight is settled, feet are static, or your own posture is leaning backward.
keyPoints: Time the sweep to the moment the foot leaves the ground
Kuzushi (balance break) with hands before the sweep lands
Sweep the ankle — not the shin
Follow through on the direction of the fall

techniques.name: Hiza-guruma
techniques.description: Directional turn throw; Teaching steering, wheel action, and forward kuzushi
avoidWhen: Do not jam the knee recklessly or throw when your block is late and uke has already planted solidly.
keyPoints: Block at the knee — not below or above
Pull-lift action with both hands creates the wheel
Rotate uke over the blocked knee
Strong tsurikomi (lift-pull) with the sleeve hand

techniques.name: Sasae-tsurikomi-ashi
techniques.description: Forward-lifting directional block; Entry timing, steering, and stopping forward stepping
avoidWhen: Do not confuse it with a kick or hard shin block; avoid using it when you cannot create lift-pull kuzushi first.
keyPoints: Prop the ankle — not kick the shin
Lift-pull (tsurikomi) with both hands simultaneously
Precise foot placement against the advancing ankle
Drive uke forward over the propped foot

techniques.name: Uki-goshi
techniques.description: Introductory hip throw; Teaches partial hip loading, torso connection, and rotational finish
avoidWhen: Do not over-commit your hips too deep like a full O-goshi when the lesson is light loading and timing.
keyPoints: Float — not full hip load (uki = floating)
Rotate uke over the side of the hip
Arm wrap creates close connection
Kuzushi pulls uke onto the hip

techniques.name: O-soto-gari
techniques.description: High-percentage backward throw; Strong for novice curriculum, self-defense entries, and big directional commitment
avoidWhen: Do not throw your chest backward or reap without first controlling uke's upper body and balance line.
keyPoints: Drive uke's weight back onto the target leg first
Chest-to-chest with outside leg reaping through
Kuzushi is backward and slightly diagonal
Reap through — not into — the leg

techniques.name: O-goshi
techniques.description: Core hip-loading throw; Excellent for learning hip placement, connection, and center-line control
avoidWhen: Do not bend at the waist to lift with the back; avoid trying to muscle a bigger uke without proper hip entry and kuzushi.
keyPoints: Load the full hip — uke rides across
Enter with pivot (tai-sabaki)
Pull with both hands to break balance forward
Drive hips back through the throw

techniques.name: O-uchi-gari
techniques.description: Pressure-forward then reap-inside; Effective against square stances and retreating balance
avoidWhen: Do not over-reach with the reaping leg when your head and grips are disconnected from uke's center.
keyPoints: Enter deep — reaping leg goes between uke's legs
Kuzushi is backward and slightly inward
Chest stays connected throughout
Follow uke down to the mat

techniques.name: Seoi-nage
techniques.description: Classic forward shoulder throw; Teaches turning entry, close connection, and load-on-back mechanics
avoidWhen: Do not drop your posture prematurely or spin in without first creating forward kuzushi and arm capture.
keyPoints: Turn in deeply — back to uke's chest
Capture uke's arm at the elbow
Load uke onto both shoulders
Pull down with arms as you bend and drive

level.name: Intermediate — Dai-nikyo (Throws 9-16)
level.description: Combination attacks, counters, and the second 8 Gokyo throws.

techniques.name: Ko-soto-gari
techniques.description: Smaller outside reap; Entry, combination, reaction tool, and posture-breaking follow-up
avoidWhen: Do not over-swing the leg or assume it will work without upper-body control and a backward weight line.
keyPoints: Lighter contact than O-soto — heel of foot, not full leg
Strong backward kuzushi creates the throw
Works as combination follow-up to O-soto
Aim for uke's heel, not the calf

techniques.name: Ko-uchi-gari
techniques.description: Inside foot attack; Strong as a setup, reaction check, and anti-square-stance tool
avoidWhen: Do not hang your upper body over the target leg or chase the foot without first managing balance and posture.
keyPoints: Sweep the inside of the ankle
Diagonal backward kuzushi with hands
Small movement — timing-driven
Often combined with O-uchi-gari

techniques.name: Koshi-guruma
techniques.description: Wheel-type hip throw; Teaching strong rotational entry and upper-body wrapping control
avoidWhen: Do not crank the neck carelessly in drilling; adapt grips safely and avoid forcing the head position.
keyPoints: Wrap arm around uke's neck (not a choke)
Hip loading similar to O-goshi
Rotate uke over the loaded hip
Safety note: never tighten the neck wrap

techniques.name: Tsurikomi-goshi
techniques.description: Grip-sensitive hip throw emphasizing lift with the lapel side; Refined kuzushi and precise hand action
avoidWhen: Do not flatten the action into a generic hip toss; it needs clear lifting-pulling mechanics and posture.
keyPoints: High lapel grip drives the lift
Hip entry is lighter than O-goshi
Strong tsurikomi (lift-pull) differentiates this from O-goshi
Maintain upright posture throughout

techniques.name: Okuri-ashi-harai
techniques.description: Movement-driven lateral sweep; Excellent for timing, angling, and intercepting side movement
avoidWhen: Do not attempt it against completely dead feet; it works on motion, not static resistance.
keyPoints: Create lateral movement with footwork first
Sweep BOTH feet simultaneously as they come together
Direction of sweep follows uke's movement
Timing is everything — cannot force it

techniques.name: Tai-otoshi
techniques.description: Classic hand-technique body drop; Teaches turn, block line, and strong forward pull
avoidWhen: Do not try to leg-block violently at knee level or throw without full-body directional control.
keyPoints: Block line with extended leg — not a trip
Drive uke forward and over the block
Turn body to create the angle
Arms create strong forward kuzushi

techniques.name: Harai-goshi
techniques.description: Dynamic sweeping hip throw; Combines hip loading with leg sweeping rhythm
avoidWhen: Do not use it before the student understands balance, hip contact, and safe sweeping height.
keyPoints: Enter like O-goshi — hip load first
Sweeping leg goes across uke's thighs — not knees
Continue sweeping through to complete the throw
Upper body drive matches the sweep timing

techniques.name: Uchi-mata
techniques.description: Major forward throw — high-value in sport and advanced standing exchange; Teaches central lift and turn
avoidWhen: Do not rush it for brand-new students without strong posture, turning ability, and safe falling skills.
keyPoints: Lift uke's inner thigh with sweeping leg
Turn-in is critical — back toward uke
Upper body drive creates the arc
Can be done from various grips — very adaptable

level.name: Advanced — Dai-sankyo (Throws 17-20)
level.description: Dynamic throws, sacrifice techniques, and upper Gokyo entries.

techniques.name: Ko-soto-gake
techniques.description: Backward hooking action; Useful as close-range reaction tool and contrast to Ko-soto-gari
avoidWhen: Do not blur hook and reap mechanics together; teach the difference clearly and watch twisting pressure.
keyPoints: Hook — not sweep — the ankle
Drive backward pressure from hands
Keep own posture upright
Compare/contrast with Ko-soto-gari (reap vs hook)

techniques.name: Tsuri-goshi
techniques.description: Belt-lifting hip throw; Teaching back-of-belt connection, lift, and close hip mechanics
avoidWhen: Do not teach it without discussing grip legality/context and safe gi-specific application.
keyPoints: Grip the back of uke's belt
Lift and load onto hip simultaneously
Hip entry stays close
Consider ruleset context for belt grip

techniques.name: Yoko-otoshi
techniques.description: Sacrifice throw introducing lateral dropping mechanics; Sideward balance breaking
avoidWhen: Do not teach it before solid ukemi and spatial awareness; poor sacrifice-throw timing can create chaos fast.
keyPoints: Drop to the side — sacrifice own position
Pull uke over you as you fall
Land on side, not back
Requires confident ukemi to practice safely

techniques.name: Ashi-guruma
techniques.description: Wheel action with a larger leg frame; Understanding rotational throws and transition from reaping to wheel concepts
avoidWhen: Do not mistime the wheel contact or treat it like O-soto-gari; it needs rotational control, not brute backward force.
keyPoints: Leg creates a fulcrum — uke rotates over it
Larger wheel arc than Hiza-guruma
Both hands drive uke's rotation
Compare: Hiza-guruma (knee), Ashi-guruma (leg), O-guruma (thigh)
